options.text: Downloads providers, initializes backend, and prepares the working directory
Shows a preview of infrastructure changes

questions.explanation: terraform init downloads provider plugins from the Terraform registry, initializes the backend (for state storage), and creates the .terraform directory. It must be run before any other commands when setting up a new configuration or changing providers.

HCL stands for HashiCorp Configuration Language. It's a declarative language designed to be human-readable and machine-friendly. It supports JSON as a compatible syntax. Terraform, Packer, and Vault all use HCL for their configuration files.

questions.text: How do you reference a variable named 'region' in HCL?
questions.explanation: In Terraform HCL, variables are referenced with the 'var.' prefix: var.region. String interpolation uses ${}: "us-${var.region}-1". Local values use local. (singular): local.my_value. Resource attributes use resource_type.resource_name.attribute.

questions.text: What is the purpose of 'terraform plan'?

options.text: Shows a preview of what Terraform will create, modify, or destroy
Generates a cost estimate for resources

questions.explanation: terraform plan compares your configuration against the current state and shows an execution plan — which resources will be added (+), changed (~), or destroyed (-). It does not make any actual changes. Save the plan with -out=plan.tfplan for reliable apply.

questions.text: What block type defines reusable computed values in Terraform?
questions.explanation: The 'locals' block defines local values — computed expressions that can be reused throughout a module. Unlike variables (user-supplied input), locals are defined within the module and not exposed externally. Reference them with local.name (singular).

questions.text: Which file extension do Terraform configuration files use?
questions.explanation: Terraform configuration files use the .tf extension (e.g., main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf). JSON configurations use .tf.json. Terraform loads all .tf files in the current directory as a single configuration. The .tfvars extension is used for variable value files.

questions.text: How do you add a validation rule to a Terraform input variable in version 1.13?
questions.explanation: Validation blocks inside variable declarations use condition (a boolean expression) and error_message (shown on failure). Terraform 1.13 allows error_message to reference var.name and use multi-line heredoc syntax for richer error messages.

options.text: Destroys all resources managed in the current state

questions.explanation: terraform destroy removes all resources managed by the current Terraform configuration (those tracked in state). It shows a plan first and requires confirmation. Use -target=resource_type.name to destroy specific resources. This is irreversible.

questions.text: What file stores the variable values for a Terraform run?
questions.explanation: terraform.tfvars (automatically loaded) or any .tfvars file (passed with -var-file=) contains variable values. The terraform.tfvars and *.auto.tfvars files are loaded automatically. Variables can also be set with TF_VAR_name environment variables or -var on the command line.

questions.text: What does the 'description' argument in an output block do?

options.text: Sets the output variable's default value
Provides documentation shown in module registry and terraform show
Restricts which resources can use the output

questions.explanation: The description in output blocks provides documentation that appears in the Terraform registry (for published modules), in 'terraform output' help text, and in IDE tooling. It's a best practice to document all outputs, especially for shared modules, so consumers know what the value represents.
